comment_text.sn8.5:2.1: While this sounds like a standard teaching, it is only found in this context. A different set of five factors is at AN 5.198.
comment_text.sn8.5:7.1: The truth is undying because the principles of the Dhamma are always true (SN 12.20:2.3). To put it another way, it is never false to say that conditioned things are impermanent.
comment_text.sn8.5:7.2: “Undying speech” (amṛtā vāco) is found in Vedic context (eg. Jaimini Gṛhyasūtra 2.1, Brahma Purāṇa 219.58), where it means speech that is immortal since it is of the Divine.
comment_text.sn8.5:7.4: The Sanskrit version of this verse translates as, “Truth may be the undying speech, for true speech is unexcelled. Truth and speech are grounded in the meaning and teaching.” (Uv 8.14). See too Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa 1.2.1.25.4, amṛte satye pratiṣṭhitām.

root_text.sn8.5:2.1: “Catūhi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgatā vācā subhāsitā hoti, no dubbhāsitā; anavajjā ca ananuvajjā ca viññūnaṁ.
root_text.sn8.5:2.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu subhāsitaṁyeva bhāsati no dubbhāsitaṁ, dhammaṁyeva bhāsati no adhammaṁ, piyaṁyeva bhāsati no appiyaṁ, saccaṁyeva bhāsati no alikaṁ.
root_text.sn8.5:2.4: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, catūhi aṅgehi samannāgatā vācā subhāsitā hoti, no dubbhāsitā, anavajjā ca ananuvajjā ca viññūnan”ti.
root_text.sn8.5:2.6: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
root_text.sn8.5:4.1: Atha kho āyasmā vaṅgīso uṭṭhāyāsanā ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yena bhagavā tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn8.5:4.4: Atha kho āyasmā vaṅgīso bhagavantaṁ sammukhā sāruppāhi gāthāhi abhitthavi:

translation_text.sn8.5:1.2: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn8.5:2.1: “Mendicants, speech that has four factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people.
translation_text.sn8.5:2.3: It’s when a mendicant speaks well, not poorly; their speech is principled, not unprincipled; they speak pleasantly, not unpleasantly; and they speak truthfully, not falsely.
translation_text.sn8.5:2.4: Speech with these four factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people.”
translation_text.sn8.5:2.5: That is what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn8.5:2.6: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:
translation_text.sn8.5:3.1: “Good people say well-spoken words are foremost;
translation_text.sn8.5:3.2: second, speech ought be principled, not unprincipled;
translation_text.sn8.5:3.4: and fourth, speak truthfully, not falsely.”
translation_text.sn8.5:4.1: Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa got up from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the Buddha, and said,
translation_text.sn8.5:4.2: “I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!”
translation_text.sn8.5:4.3: “Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn8.5:4.4: Then Vaṅgīsa extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses:
translation_text.sn8.5:5.4: such speech is truly well spoken.
translation_text.sn8.5:6.4: that bring nothing bad to others.
translation_text.sn8.5:7.3: Good people say the teaching and meaning
translation_text.sn8.5:8.1: The words spoken by the Buddha
translation_text.sn8.5:8.3: for making an end of suffering:
translation_text.sn8.5:8.4: this really is the best kind of speech.”
